questionList.choiceQuestionContent:

Negative

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The plates of an isolated, charged parallel-plate capacitor are moved apart. Which of the following correctly indicates how the capacitance of the capacitor changes and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: Decreases, because the amount of charge on the plates of the capacitor decreases.
Decreases, because the potential difference across the capacitor plates increases.

Does not change, because the area of the capacitor plates does not change.

D .

questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A student is examining two nails, labeled X and Y. When Nail X is connected to a magnet, it can pick up iron filings, but drops roughly half of them when the magnet is removed. When Nail Y is connected to a magnet, it also picks up iron filings, but it loses all of them when the magnet is removed.

Which of the following provides a correct description of the types of materials in these nails and provides valid reasoning?

  Nail X Nail Y Reason
A Ferromagnetic Ferromagnetic Both materials become magnetized in the presence of a magnet.
B Ferromagnetic Paramagnetic A ferromagnetic material retains some of the strength of the magnetic field after the magnet is removed. A paramagnetic material loses all its magnetic field strength when the magnet is removed.
C Paramagnetic Ferromagnetic A paramagnetic material retains some of the strength of the magnetic field after the magnet is removed. A ferromagnetic material loses all its magnetic field strength when the magnet is removed.
D Paramagnetic Paramagnetic Both materials become magnetized and drop some of the iron filings when the magnet is removed.

B
C
D

A .

B .

B

C .

C

D .

D

questionList.questionTitle: Magnetic fields - acceleration of conducting bar
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
At the instant shown, a conducting bar is sliding to the right along frictionless, conducting rails while maintaining contact with the rails. The rails are connected to a resistor, as shown. The rails and sliding bar are in a magnetic field that is directed into the page.

Which of the following correctly describes the acceleration of the bar and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: The acceleration is zero because the resistor is not in the magnetic field.
The acceleration is zero because the magnetic force creates a charge separation across the ends of the bar but does not oppose the motion of the bar.
The acceleration is nonzero because there will be a current in the bar due to the induced emf and the magnetic field will exert a force on the current-carrying bar.
The acceleration is nonzero because the electric field created by the charge separation in the bar will exert a force on the bar.

questionOption:
A .

The acceleration is zero because the resistor is not in the magnetic field.

B .

The acceleration is zero because the magnetic force creates a charge separation across the ends of the bar but does not oppose the motion of the bar.

C .

The acceleration is nonzero because there will be a current in the bar due to the induced emf and the magnetic field will exert a force on the current-carrying bar.

D .

The acceleration is nonzero because the electric field created by the charge separation in the bar will exert a force on the bar.

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

The circuit shown in the diagram in Figure 1 consists of a battery with emf Ɛ, a switch, two identical resistors of resistance R, and a capacitor of capacitance C, as shown. The switch is initially open, and the capacitor is uncharged.

(a) The switch is then closed.

  • i. On the circuit diagram in Figure 2, draw an arrow next to each resistor to show the direction of the initial current in each resistor immediately after the switch is closed. If there is no current in either of the resistors, write “ I = 0 ” next to that resistor.

• ii. Derive an equation for the initial current in the battery immediately after the switch is closed. Express your answer in terms of Ɛ and R. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

• iii. After the switch has been closed for a long time, the switch is opened at time t=0. Using integral calculus, derive an expression to show that the charge on the positive capacitor plate as a function of time as the capacitor discharges is equal to q(t)=CƐe−t2RC. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

(b) Derive an expression for the time t1/2 at which the current in the branch with the discharging capacitor is half the current immediately after the switch is opened. Express your answer in terms of R and C. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

The circuit shown in the diagram in Figure 1 consists of a battery with emf Ɛ, a switch, two identical resistors of resistance R, and a capacitor of capacitance C, as shown. The switch is initially open, and the capacitor is uncharged.

(a) The switch is then closed.

  • i. On the circuit diagram in Figure 2, draw an arrow next to each resistor to show the direction of the initial current in each resistor immediately after the switch is closed. If there is no current in either of the resistors, write “ I = 0 ” next to that resistor.

• ii. Derive an equation for the initial current in the battery immediately after the switch is closed. Express your answer in terms of Ɛ and R. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

• iii. After the switch has been closed for a long time, the switch is opened at time t=0. Using integral calculus, derive an expression to show that the charge on the positive capacitor plate as a function of time as the capacitor discharges is equal to q(t)=CƐe−t2RC. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

(b) Derive an expression for the time t1/2 at which the current in the branch with the discharging capacitor is half the current immediately after the switch is opened. Express your answer in terms of R and C. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

The circuit shown in the diagram in Figure 1 consists of a battery with emf Ɛ, a switch, two identical resistors of resistance R, and a capacitor of capacitance C, as shown. The switch is initially open, and the capacitor is uncharged.

(a) The switch is then closed.

  • i. On the circuit diagram in Figure 2, draw an arrow next to each resistor to show the direction of the initial current in each resistor immediately after the switch is closed. If there is no current in either of the resistors, write “ I = 0 ” next to that resistor.

• ii. Derive an equation for the initial current in the battery immediately after the switch is closed. Express your answer in terms of Ɛ and R. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

• iii. After the switch has been closed for a long time, the switch is opened at time t=0. Using integral calculus, derive an expression to show that the charge on the positive capacitor plate as a function of time as the capacitor discharges is equal to q(t)=CƐe−t2RC. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

(b) Derive an expression for the time t1/2 at which the current in the branch with the discharging capacitor is half the current immediately after the switch is opened. Express your answer in terms of R and C. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

The circuit shown in the diagram in Figure 1 consists of a battery with emf Ɛ, a switch, two identical resistors of resistance R, and a capacitor of capacitance C, as shown. The switch is initially open, and the capacitor is uncharged.

Using integral calculus, derive an expression to show that the charge on the positive capacitor plate as a function of time as the capacitor discharges is equal to q(t)=CƐe−t2RC. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

(b) Derive an expression for the time t1/2 at which the current in the branch with the discharging capacitor is half the current immediately after the switch is opened. Express your answer in terms of R and C. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

A charged nonconducting solid sphere of radius R has charge +Q uniformly distributed throughout the volume of the sphere. An uncharged metal conducting spherical shell of inner radius 2R and outer radius 3R is concentric with the sphere, as shown in Figure 1 .

 

(a) Figure 2 shows the sphere, shell, and four dots. On each dot, draw an arrow to indicate the direction, if any, of the electric field at that point. If the electric field is zero at any of the dots, write “E=0” next to the dot.

(b) Derive an expression for the magnitude E of the electric field inside the solid sphere at a distance r from the center of the solid sphere, where r<R. Express your answer in terms of Q, r, R, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
(c) On the axes provided, sketch a graph of the magnitude E of the electric field as a function of the distance r from the center of the solid sphere for 0<r<4R.
(d) The nonconducting sphere of charge +Q is replaced with a conducting sphere of charge +Q. Indicate how one feature of the graph in part (c) would change. Briefly justify your reasoning.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


In Experiment 1, students are asked to use a graph to determine the magnitude B of a uniform external magnetic field. The students have access to a rod with known resistance R that is partially in the uniform external magnetic field. The region has width d, as shown. The rod is suspended from a force sensor and the ends of the rod are connected to a power supply with variable emf. The mass of the rod, the mass of the wires, and the magnitude of Earth’s magnetic field are negligible.

(a) Describe a procedure for collecting data that would allow the students to use a graph to determine the magnitude B of the uniform external magnetic field. Include any steps necessary to reduce experimental uncertainty.
(b) Describe how the collected data could be graphed and how that graph would be analyzed to determine the magnitude B of the uniform external magnetic field.

In Experiment 2, the external magnetic field is removed, and the students hold a second current-carrying rod parallel to the rod attached to the force sensor. Both rods have the same unknown current I. The students measure the force per unit length Fℓ exerted on the top rod for various distances r between the rods. The students are asked to use a graph to experimentally determine the current I. The table provides Fℓ for each value of r.

Fℓ(×10-8N/m)r(m)
7.90.10
4.20.20
2.60.30
2.30.40
1.80.50

(c)

  • i. Indicate what measured and/or calculated quantities could be graphed to yield a straight line that could be used to determine I. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

    Vertical axis: __________          Horizontal axis: __________
• ii. Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) on the graph provided. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units, as appropriate.
• iii. Draw the best-fit line for the data graphed in (c)(ii).
(d) Using the best-fit line, calculate I.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

questionList.questionTitle: Induction - bar on slanted rails in B field

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

A conducting bar of mass m is placed on two long conducting rails a distance ℓ apart. The rails are inclined at an angle θ with respect to the horizontal and the bar is able to slide on the rails with negligible friction between the bar and rails. The bar and rails are in a uniform and constant magnetic field of magnitude B oriented perpendicular to the rails. A resistor of resistance R connects the upper ends of the rails and completes the circuit, as shown. The bar is released from rest at the top of the incline.

(a) Indicate whether the magnitude of the acceleration of the bar increases, decreases, or stays the same as the bar begins moving down the rails.

_____ Increases          _____ Decreases          _____ Stays the same

Justify your selection without manipulating equations.

(b) Some time after its release, the bar is moving with speed v down the rails. Derive an equation for the acceleration of the bar in terms of m, ℓ, θ, B, R, v, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
(c) Indicate whether your equation for the acceleration of the bar derived in part (b) is consistent with your reasoning in part (a)? Briefly justify your answer.

partList.directionsText:
PHYSICS C: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM
SECTION I
Time—45 minutes
35 Questions
Directions: Each of the questions or incomplete statements below is followed by five suggested answers or completions. Select the one that is best in each case and then fill in the corresponding circle on the answer sheet.

PHYSICS C: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETTSM
SECTION II
Time—45 minutes
3 Questions
Directions: Answer all three questions. The suggested time is sbout 15 minutes for answering each of the questions, which are worth 15 points each. The parts within a question may not have equal weight. Show all your work in this booklet in the spaces provided after each part.
